title: Merge k arrays
description: Merge k sorted arrays using min-heap

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate total elements = 3 arrays * 3 elements each = 9 elements
Step 2: Create result array of size 9
Step 3: Copy all elements: result = [1,4,7,2,5,8,3,6,9]
Step 4: Sort result using quicksort/mergesort:
  Initial: [1,4,7,2,5,8,3,6,9]
  After sort: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]
Step 5: Return sorted array

Step 1: Total elements = 2 arrays * 3 elements = 6
Step 2: Create result array size 6
Step 3: Copy elements: result = [10,20,30,5,15,25]
Step 4: Sort result:
  Initial: [10,20,30,5,15,25]
  After sort: [5,10,15,20,25,30]
Step 5: Return sorted array

approaches.name: Brute Force O(nk log nk)
approaches.summary: Combine all k arrays into one large array and sort it using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs, as it ignores the sorted nature of the individual arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge k sorted arrays using brute force approach
ladders.desc: Combine all k sorted arrays into one large array and sort it using standard sorting algorithm
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to merging k sorted arrays is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs. The intuition is simple: since we need all elements in sorted order, we can collect all elements from all arrays into one large array and then sort that array using a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. This approach completely ignores the fact that the individual arrays are already sorted, which is why it's inefficient. However, it's conceptually simple and works correctly for any input. The algorithm has three main steps: 1) Calculate total number of elements across all arrays (n*k where each array has n elements). 2) Create a result array of size n*k. 3) Copy all elements from all input arrays into the result array. 4) Sort the result array using any O(n log n) sorting algorithm. The time complexity is O(nk log nk) because we're sorting nk elements. The space complexity is O(nk) for storing the result array. This approach connects to more efficient solutions by serving as a baseline - any better solution must beat this time complexity. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays, incorrectly calculating total size, and using inefficient sorting algorithms. The key insight is recognizing that while this works, it's suboptimal because it doesn't leverage the pre-sorted nature of input arrays.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate total elements = 3 arrays * 3 elements each = 9 elements
Step 2: Create result array of size 9
Step 3: Copy all elements: result = [1,4,7,2,5,8,3,6,9]
Step 4: Sort result using quicksort/mergesort:
  Initial: [1,4,7,2,5,8,3,6,9]
  After sort: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]
Step 5: Return sorted array

Step 1: Total elements = 2 arrays * 3 elements = 6
Step 2: Create result array size 6
Step 3: Copy elements: result = [10,20,30,5,15,25]
Step 4: Sort result:
  Initial: [10,20,30,5,15,25]
  After sort: [5,10,15,20,25,30]
Step 5: Return sorted array

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Combine all arrays into one large array
ladders.desc: Flatten k arrays into a single array by concatenating all elements
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the first critical step in the brute force approach. The intuition is that before we can sort all elements, we need to collect them into a single data structure. This involves iterating through each of the k arrays and copying all their elements into a new result array. The algorithm is: 1) Calculate total number of elements by summing lengths of all arrays. 2) Create an empty result array with capacity equal to total elements. 3) For each input array, copy all its elements to the result array. 4) Return the combined array. Time complexity is O(nk) where n is average array length, since we visit each element once. Space complexity is O(nk) for the result array. This feeds into the full problem by providing the unsorted combined array that will then be sorted. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in array indices, forgetting to handle empty arrays, and inefficient concatenation. The key insight is that this operation is linear in the total number of elements, which is optimal for the combining step - the inefficiency comes from the subsequent sorting, not from this combining operation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate total elements = 3 + 3 = 6
Step 2: Create result array of size 6
Step 3: Copy first array [1,2,3] → result[0:3] = [1,2,3]
Step 4: Copy second array [4,5,6] → result[3:6] = [4,5,6]
Step 5: Result = [1,2,3,4,5,6]

Step 1: Total elements = 2 + 1 + 3 = 6
Step 2: Create result array size 6
Step 3: Copy [10,20] → result[0:2] = [10,20]
Step 4: Copy [30] → result[2:3] = [30]
Step 5: Copy [40,50,60] → result[3:6] = [40,50,60]
Step 6: Result = [10,20,30,40,50,60]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sorting a combined array using standard algorithm
ladders.desc: Apply O(n log n) sorting algorithm to an unsorted array
ladders.explanation: This is the core computational step that gives the brute force approach its time complexity. The intuition is that once we have all elements in one array, we can use any efficient comparison-based sorting algorithm to arrange them in order. The most common choices are quicksort (average O(n log n)) or mergesort (guaranteed O(n log n)). The algorithm depends on the specific sort chosen, but generally follows: 1) If array has 0 or 1 elements, return it (base case). 2) Choose a pivot (quicksort) or split array (mergesort). 3) Recursively sort subarrays. 4) Combine results. Time complexity is O(m log m) where m is array size. Space complexity is O(m) for mergesort or O(log m) for quicksort (call stack). This connects to the full problem by being the step that actually produces the sorted output after arrays are combined. Common mistakes include: implementing inefficient O(n²) sorts, not handling edge cases (empty arrays, already sorted arrays), and recursion depth issues. The key insight is that while sorting is necessary, doing it on the combined array ignores valuable information - the individual arrays were already sorted, which could be exploited for better efficiency.

examples.trace:
Using mergesort:
Step 1: Split [4,2,7,1,5] → [4,2,7] and [1,5]
Step 2: Sort [4,2,7] → split [4,2] and [7]
  Sort [4,2] → split [4] and [2] → merge [2,4]
  Merge [2,4] with [7] → [2,4,7]
Step 3: Sort [1,5] → split [1] and [5] → merge [1,5]
Step 4: Merge [2,4,7] with [1,5] → [1,2,4,5,7]

Using quicksort (pivot=first element):
Step 1: Pivot=10, array=[10,30,20,50,40]
  Left of pivot: []
  Right of pivot: [30,20,50,40]
Step 2: Sort right [30,20,50,40], pivot=30
  Left: [20]
  Right: [50,40]
  Sort [50,40], pivot=50 → left:[40], right:[]
  Combine: [40,50]
  Combine: [20,30,40,50]
Step 3: Combine with pivot: [] + [10] + [20,30,40,50] = [10,20,30,40,50]

ladders.title: Building Block — Array concatenation and memory allocation
ladders.desc: Combine multiple arrays into one with proper memory management
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating a new array that contains all elements from multiple source arrays. The intuition is that we need a contiguous block of memory to hold all elements before sorting. The algorithm: 1) Calculate total size by summing lengths of all input arrays. 2) Allocate a new array of that size. 3) For each source array, copy its elements to the appropriate position in the destination array. 4) Keep track of current insertion position. Time complexity is O(total elements) since each element is copied once. Space complexity is O(total elements) for the new array. This connects to level 2 by providing the unsorted array that will be sorted. Common mistakes include: not pre-allocating array (leading to repeated resizing), incorrect index calculations, and shallow copying issues with object arrays. The key insight is that this operation is fundamentally about memory management - we need to allocate exactly enough space and copy elements efficiently. In languages with dynamic arrays, this might involve appending, but pre-allocation is more efficient.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate total size = 2 + 3 = 5
Step 2: Allocate result array of size 5
Step 3: Start position = 0
Step 4: Copy first array [1,2]:
  result[0] = 1
  result[1] = 2
  position = 2
Step 5: Copy second array [3,4,5]:
  result[2] = 3
  result[3] = 4
  result[4] = 5
Step 6: Result = [1,2,3,4,5]

Step 1: Total size = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4
Step 2: Allocate result size 4
Step 3: position = 0
Step 4: Copy [10]: result[0]=10, position=1
Step 5: Copy [20,30]: result[1]=20, result[2]=30, position=3
Step 6: Copy [40]: result[3]=40
Step 7: Result = [10,20,30,40]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating through arrays and copying elements
ladders.desc: Fundamental loop operation to traverse and copy array contents
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic operation needed: looping through an array and accessing/copying each element. The intuition is simple - to process arrays, we need to visit each element sequentially. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an index variable to 0. 2) While index is less than array length, access array[index]. 3) Perform operation (like copy to another array). 4) Increment index. Time complexity is O(n) for an array of length n. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself (not counting any output). This connects to level 3 as the mechanism used to copy elements from source to destination arrays. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using <= instead of <), modifying array while iterating, and not handling empty arrays. The key insight is that array iteration is a fundamental programming pattern that appears in almost all array algorithms. Understanding how to correctly traverse arrays is prerequisite to any array manipulation task.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Create destination array of size 3
Step 2: i=0, array[0]=1 → dest[0]=1
Step 3: i=1, array[1]=2 → dest[1]=2
Step 4: i=2, array[2]=3 → dest[2]=3
Step 5: i=3 (stop, since 3 >= length 3)
Step 6: Destination = [1,2,3]

examples.input: array=[10,20,30,40], copy to positions 2-5 in larger array
examples.trace:
Step 1: Larger array has positions 0-7
Step 2: i=0, source[0]=10 → larger[2]=10
Step 3: i=1, source[1]=20 → larger[3]=20
Step 4: i=2, source[2]=30 → larger[4]=30
Step 5: i=3, source[3]=40 → larger[5]=40
Step 6: i=4 (stop)
Step 7: Larger array now has values at indices 2-5

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding arrays and their properties
ladders.desc: Fundamental concept of arrays as contiguous memory blocks with indices
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation: understanding what an array is. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access to any element if you know its index. Key properties: 1) Fixed size (in many languages) or dynamically resizable. 2) Zero-based indexing (first element at index 0). 3) Contiguous memory allocation. 4) Homogeneous elements (same data type). Basic operations: declaration, initialization, accessing elements via index, modifying elements. Time complexity: access O(1), search O(n), insertion/deletion O(n) at arbitrary positions. Space complexity: O(n) for n elements. This connects to level 4 as the fundamental structure being iterated. Common beginner mistakes: confusing index 0 with 1, accessing out of bounds, not understanding that arrays are reference types in some languages. The key insight is that arrays are the most basic collection type and understanding them is essential for any algorithm that processes sequences of data. Everything builds from this foundation.

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements:
Index 0 → value 10
Index 1 → value 20
Index 2 → value 30
Index 3 → value 40
Length = 4
Valid indices: 0, 1, 2, 3
Access examples:
arr[0] = 10
arr[2] = 30
arr[3] = 40
arr[4] would be ERROR (out of bounds)

Step 1: Declare array of size 3
Step 2: Initialize values:
  arr[0] = 5
  arr[1] = 10
  arr[2] = 15
Step 3: Resulting array:
  Index 0: 5
  Index 1: 10
  Index 2: 15
Step 4: Total elements = 3
Step 5: Memory layout: contiguous blocks storing 5, then 10, then 15

approaches.name: Sequential Merge O(nk²)
approaches.summary: Merge the k arrays one by one using a two-pointer merge technique, similar to merging two sorted arrays repeatedly. This approach is simple but becomes slow as k increases, as each merge operation processes elements multiple times.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge k sorted arrays using sequential two-pointer merging
ladders.desc: Complete implementation merging k sorted arrays by repeatedly merging two arrays at a time using the two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: The sequential merge approach solves the problem by treating it as a series of two-array merge operations. We start with the first array as our initial result, then merge it with the second array to create a new merged array. This new merged array becomes our result, which we then merge with the third array, and so on until all k arrays are processed. The intuition is that merging sorted arrays is associative - we can merge them in any order and get the same final sorted result. This approach leverages the simple, well-understood two-array merge algorithm repeatedly. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize result as the first array. 2. For each remaining array from index 1 to k-1: a. Create a temporary array to store the merged result of current result and the next array. b. Use two pointers (i for result, j for next array) starting at 0. c. While both pointers are within bounds: compare elements at result[i] and next_array[j], append the smaller to temporary array, and increment that pointer. d. Append remaining elements from whichever array still has elements. e. Set result = temporary array. 3. Return result. Time complexity is O(nk²) because each element gets merged multiple times - the first array elements get merged k-1 times, second array elements get merged k-2 times, etc. Space complexity is O(nk) for storing intermediate merged arrays. This connects to L1 as it uses the two-array merge subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays, not resetting pointers correctly for each merge, and inefficient array copying. Real code pattern: def merge_k_arrays(arrays): result = arrays[0] for i in range(1, len(arrays)): temp = [] p1, p2 = 0, 0 while p1 < len(result) and p2 < len(arrays[i]): if result[p1] <= arrays[i][p2]: temp.append(result[p1]); p1 += 1 else: temp.append(arrays[i][p2]); p2 += 1 while p1 < len(result): temp.append(result[p1]); p1 += 1 while p2 < len(arrays[i]): temp.append(arrays[i][p2]); p2 += 1 result = temp return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: result = [1,4,7]
Step 2: Merge result with [2,5,8]:
  Compare 1 vs 2 → add 1 (temp=[1])
  Compare 4 vs 2 → add 2 (temp=[1,2])
  Compare 4 vs 5 → add 4 (temp=[1,2,4])
  Compare 7 vs 5 → add 5 (temp=[1,2,4,5])
  Compare 7 vs 8 → add 7 (temp=[1,2,4,5,7])
  Add remaining 8 (temp=[1,2,4,5,7,8])
  result = [1,2,4,5,7,8]
Step 3: Merge result with [3,6,9]:
  Compare 1 vs 3 → add 1 (temp=[1])
  Compare 2 vs 3 → add 2 (temp=[1,2])
  Compare 4 vs 3 → add 3 (temp=[1,2,3])
  Compare 4 vs 6 → add 4 (temp=[1,2,3,4])
  Compare 5 vs 6 → add 5 (temp=[1,2,3,4,5])
  Compare 7 vs 6 → add 6 (temp=[1,2,3,4,5,6])
  Compare 7 vs 9 → add 7 (temp=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7])
  Compare 8 vs 9 → add 8 (temp=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8])
  Add remaining 9 (temp=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9])
  result = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]
Final: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]

Step 1: result = [10,20,30]
Step 2: Merge result with [15,25]:
  Compare 10 vs 15 → add 10 (temp=[10])
  Compare 20 vs 15 → add 15 (temp=[10,15])
  Compare 20 vs 25 → add 20 (temp=[10,15,20])
  Compare 30 vs 25 → add 25 (temp=[10,15,20,25])
  Add remaining 30 (temp=[10,15,20,25,30])
  result = [10,15,20,25,30]
Step 3: Merge result with [5,35,45,55]:
  Compare 10 vs 5 → add 5 (temp=[5])
  Compare 10 vs 35 → add 10 (temp=[5,10])
  Compare 15 vs 35 → add 15 (temp=[5,10,15])
  Compare 20 vs 35 → add 20 (temp=[5,10,15,20])
  Compare 25 vs 35 → add 25 (temp=[5,10,15,20,25])
  Compare 30 vs 35 → add 30 (temp=[5,10,15,20,25,30])
  Add remaining 35,45,55 (temp=[5,10,15,20,25,30,35,45,55])
  result = [5,10,15,20,25,30,35,45,55]
Final: [5,10,15,20,25,30,35,45,55]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge two sorted arrays using two-pointer technique
ladders.desc: Implement the fundamental merge operation that combines two sorted arrays into one sorted array.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer merge is the core subroutine that makes sequential merging possible. The intuition is simple: since both arrays are already sorted, the smallest element in the combined result must be the smaller of the first elements of each array. We maintain two pointers (indices) - one for each array - starting at position 0. At each step, we compare the elements at both pointers, take the smaller one, append it to our result, and move that pointer forward. This works because each array is sorted, so by always taking the smallest available element, we build the merged array in sorted order. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty result array. 2. Set pointers i=0 for array A, j=0 for array B. 3. While i < len(A) AND j < len(B): a. If A[i] <= B[j]: append A[i] to result, i++. b. Else: append B[j] to result, j++. 4. Append remaining elements from whichever array still has elements (while i < len(A): append A[i]; i++ OR while j < len(B): append B[j]; j++). 5. Return result. Time complexity is O(m+n) where m,n are array lengths, as we process each element exactly once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the result array. This connects to L0 as it's the building block called repeatedly in the full solution. Common mistakes include: using < instead of <= (important for stability), forgetting to handle remaining elements, and off-by-one errors in pointer increments. Real code pattern: def merge_two_arrays(arr1, arr2): result = [] i, j = 0, 0 while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i += 1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j += 1 while i < len(arr1): result.append(arr1[i]); i += 1 while j < len(arr2): result.append(arr2[j]); j += 1 return result

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential accumulation of merged results
ladders.desc: Understanding how repeatedly merging pairs builds the final k-way merged result.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of sequential merging is that we can build the final k-way merged result incrementally by maintaining a running merged result. We start with the first array as our initial result. Then for each subsequent array, we merge it with our current result to create a new, larger merged result. This works because merging is associative: (A merge B) merge C = A merge (B merge C) = the final sorted combination of all elements. The intuition is similar to how we might combine multiple sorted lists in real life - we could take two lists, merge them, then merge that result with the next list, and so on. The key realization is that we don't need to merge all arrays simultaneously; we can process them sequentially. Step-by-step: 1. Let result = arrays[0] 2. For i from 1 to k-1: result = merge(result, arrays[i]) 3. Return result. The time complexity accumulates because each merge operation processes all elements in the current result plus the new array. If each array has n elements, first merge: n+n = 2n operations, second: 2n+n = 3n, ... up to (k-1)n+n = kn. Summing gives O(nk²). Space complexity is O(nk) as we store intermediate results. This connects to L1 as it shows how to apply the two-array merge repeatedly. Common mistakes include: trying to merge in wrong order (which still works but affects performance), not understanding why this is O(nk²), and creating unnecessary copies. Real code pattern: result = arrays[0]; for i in range(1, len(arrays)): result = merge_two_arrays(result, arrays[i]); return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: result = A = [1,4]
Step 2: Merge result with B=[2,5]:
  Compare 1 vs 2 → add 1 → result=[1]
  Compare 4 vs 2 → add 2 → result=[1,2]
  Compare 4 vs 5 → add 4 → result=[1,2,4]
  Add remaining 5 → result=[1,2,4,5]
  New result = [1,2,4,5]
Step 3: Merge result with C=[3,6]:
  Compare 1 vs 3 → add 1 → temp=[1]
  Compare 2 vs 3 → add 2 → temp=[1,2]
  Compare 4 vs 3 → add 3 → temp=[1,2,3]
  Compare 4 vs 6 → add 4 → temp=[1,2,3,4]
  Compare 5 vs 6 → add 5 → temp=[1,2,3,4,5]
  Add remaining 6 → temp=[1,2,3,4,5,6]
  Final result = [1,2,3,4,5,6]

Step 1: result = [10]
Step 2: Merge result with [20]:
  Compare 10 vs 20 → add 10 → temp=[10]
  Add remaining 20 → temp=[10,20]
  result = [10,20]
Step 3: Merge result with [30]:
  Compare 10 vs 30 → add 10 → temp=[10]
  Compare 20 vs 30 → add 20 → temp=[10,20]
  Add remaining 30 → temp=[10,20,30]
  result = [10,20,30]
Step 4: Merge result with [40]:
  Compare 10 vs 40 → add 10 → temp=[10]
  Compare 20 vs 40 → add 20 → temp=[10,20]
  Compare 30 vs 40 → add 30 → temp=[10,20,30]
  Add remaining 40 → temp=[10,20,30,40]
  Final result = [10,20,30,40]

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating through multiple arrays with accumulation
ladders.desc: Master the pattern of processing a list of arrays while maintaining a running result.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches the fundamental programming pattern of iterating through a collection while accumulating a result. The intuition is that many algorithms involve taking an initial value and repeatedly combining it with each element in a collection. For merging k arrays, our initial value is the first array, and our combination operation is the merge function. We iterate through the remaining arrays, each time merging the current result with the next array to produce a new result. This is similar to how reduce/fold operations work in functional programming. Step-by-step: 1. If no arrays, return empty list. 2. Initialize accumulator = arrays[0] 3. For i from 1 to len(arrays)-1: accumulator = combine(accumulator, arrays[i]) 4. Return accumulator. The key insight is recognizing that this pattern applies here: combine = merge_two_arrays, accumulator starts as first array. Time complexity depends on the combine operation; here it's O(nk²) overall. Space complexity is O(nk) for storing intermediate results. This connects to L2 as it provides the iterative framework for sequential accumulation. Common mistakes include: not handling empty input, off-by-one errors in loop range, and modifying the original arrays unintentionally. Real code pattern: def process_arrays(arrays, combine_func): if not arrays: return [] result = arrays[0] for i in range(1, len(arrays)): result = combine_func(result, arrays[i]) return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: result = [1,2]
Step 2: combine(result, [3,4]) = concatenate → result = [1,2,3,4]
Step 3: combine(result, [5,6]) = concatenate → result = [1,2,3,4,5,6]
Final: [1,2,3,4,5,6]

Step 1: result = 10 (sum of [10])
Step 2: combine(10, [20]) = 10 + 20 = 30
Step 3: combine(30, [30]) = 30 + 30 = 60
Final: 60

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and selecting elements from two arrays
ladders.desc: Learn to compare elements from two different arrays and choose the smaller one.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is at the heart of merging: given two sorted arrays, we need to compare their current elements and select the smaller one to add to our result. The intuition is simple: since both arrays are sorted, the smallest element among all remaining elements must be the smaller of the two elements at the current positions. We maintain two index pointers, one for each array. At each step, we look at arr1[i] and arr2[j], compare them, and take the smaller value. If they're equal, we typically take from the first array for stability. After taking an element, we move that array's pointer forward. This operation repeats until one array is exhausted. Step-by-step: 1. Check if i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2). 2. If arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: take arr1[i], increment i. 3. Else: take arr2[j], increment j. 4. Add taken element to result. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1) aside from result storage. This connects to L3 as it's the core comparison within the merge operation. Common mistakes include: comparing beyond array bounds, using < instead of <= (affects handling of equal elements), and not incrementing the correct pointer. Real code pattern: if i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i += 1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j += 1

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare arr1[0]=1 vs arr2[0]=2
Step 2: 1 <= 2 is true
Step 3: Select arr1[0]=1
Step 4: Increment i to 1
Result: Selected 1, i=1, j=0

Step 1: Compare arr1[1]=20 vs arr2[0]=15
Step 2: 20 <= 15 is false
Step 3: Select arr2[0]=15
Step 4: Increment j to 1
Result: Selected 15, i=1, j=1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding sorted arrays and element comparison
ladders.desc: Learn what it means for an array to be sorted and how to compare elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the prerequisite knowledge needed for merging: understanding sorted arrays and element comparison. A sorted array is one where elements are arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is less than or equal to the next element). For example, [1,3,5] is sorted because 1 ≤ 3 ≤ 5. The intuition is that sorting organizes data so we can make efficient decisions - in a sorted array, we know the smallest element is at the beginning, and each subsequent element is at least as large as the previous one. Comparing elements means examining two values and determining their relationship: whether one is less than, equal to, or greater than the other. This is typically done with comparison operators (<, <=, >, >=, ==). For merging, we need to compare elements from different arrays to decide which one comes first in the combined sorted result. Step-by-step: 1. Identify two elements to compare (e.g., a from array A, b from array B). 2. Evaluate a <= b. 3. If true, a should come before b in sorted order. 4. If false, b should come before a. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as it provides the basic understanding needed for the compare-and-select operation. Common mistakes include: confusing sorted order (non-decreasing vs strictly increasing), not understanding that equal elements are allowed in sorted arrays, and incorrect use of comparison operators. Real code pattern: a = arr1[i]; b = arr2[j]; if a <= b: # a comes first else: # b comes first

examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify elements: a=3, b=5
Step 2: Evaluate 3 <= 5
Step 3: 3 <= 5 is true
Step 4: Therefore in sorted order, 3 should come before 5

Step 1: Identify elements: a=10, b=7
Step 2: Evaluate 10 <= 7
Step 3: 10 <= 7 is false
Step 4: Therefore in sorted order, 7 should come before 10

testCases.expected: 10 <= 10 is true, either can come first (typically first)
7 <= 7 is true, either can come first
1 <= 1 is true, either can come first

approaches.name: Min-Heap O(nk log k)
approaches.summary: Use a min-heap to efficiently track the smallest elements from each of the k arrays. Repeatedly extract the minimum from the heap and add the next element from the same array, ensuring optimal merging by leveraging the sorted order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge k Sorted Arrays Using Min-Heap
ladders.desc: Complete implementation merging k sorted arrays into one sorted array using a min-heap.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that we need to efficiently find the smallest element among all k arrays at each step. Since each array is already sorted, we only need to compare the current smallest elements from each array. A min-heap gives us O(log k) access to the minimum element, making the overall process O(nk log k) where n is the average array length. We start by pushing the first element from each array into the heap along with array index and element index. Then we repeatedly extract the minimum, add it to our result, and push the next element from the same array if available. This ensures we always process elements in sorted order while maintaining heap size k. The space complexity is O(k) for the heap. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the heap management subroutine from L1 as its core component. Common mistakes include forgetting to track array indices properly, not handling empty arrays, and incorrect heap comparisons. Real code pattern: Initialize heap with first elements, while heap not empty: pop min, add to result, push next from same array if exists.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize heap with (1,0,0), (2,1,0), (3,2,0). Heap: [(1,0,0),(2,1,0),(3,2,0)]. Result: []. Step 2: Pop min (1,0,0). Result: [1]. Push next from array 0: (4,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0),(3,2,0),(4,0,1)]. Step 3: Pop min (2,1,0). Result: [1,2]. Push next from array 1: (5,1,1). Heap: [(3,2,0),(4,0,1),(5,1,1)]. Step 4: Pop min (3,2,0). Result: [1,2,3]. Push next from array 2: (6,2,1). Heap: [(4,0,1),(5,1,1),(6,2,1)]. Step 5: Pop min (4,0,1). Result: [1,2,3,4]. Push next from array 0: (7,0,2). Heap: [(5,1,1),(6,2,1),(7,0,2)]. Step 6: Pop min (5,1,1). Result: [1,2,3,4,5]. Push next from array 1: (8,1,2). Heap: [(6,2,1),(7,0,2),(8,1,2)]. Step 7: Pop min (6,2,1). Result: [1,2,3,4,5,6]. Push next from array 2: (9,2,2). Heap: [(7,0,2),(8,1,2),(9,2,2)]. Step 8: Pop min (7,0,2). Result: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7]. Array 0 exhausted. Heap: [(8,1,2),(9,2,2)]. Step 9: Pop min (8,1,2). Result: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8]. Array 1 exhausted. Heap: [(9,2,2)]. Step 10: Pop min (9,2,2). Result: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]. Array 2 exhausted. Heap empty. Done.

Step 1: Initialize heap with (10,0,0), (15,1,0), (5,2,0). Heap: [(5,2,0),(10,0,0),(15,1,0)]. Result: []. Step 2: Pop min (5,2,0). Result: [5]. Push next from array 2: (30,2,1). Heap: [(10,0,0),(15,1,0),(30,2,1)]. Step 3: Pop min (10,0,0). Result: [5,10]. Push next from array 0: (20,0,1). Heap: [(15,1,0),(20,0,1),(30,2,1)]. Step 4: Pop min (15,1,0). Result: [5,10,15]. Push next from array 1: (25,1,1). Heap: [(20,0,1),(25,1,1),(30,2,1)]. Step 5: Pop min (20,0,1). Result: [5,10,15,20]. Array 0 exhausted. Heap: [(25,1,1),(30,2,1)]. Step 6: Pop min (25,1,1). Result: [5,10,15,20,25]. Array 1 exhausted. Heap: [(30,2,1)]. Step 7: Pop min (30,2,1). Result: [5,10,15,20,25,30]. Array 2 exhausted. Heap empty. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Min-Heap Management for k Arrays
ladders.desc: Implement the heap operations to track and update smallest elements from k arrays.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that we need a data structure that can efficiently give us the smallest element among k candidates and allow quick updates when we remove an element. A min-heap is perfect because it maintains the minimum at the root with O(1) access and supports insertion/removal in O(log k). The key insight is storing tuples (value, array_index, element_index) in the heap so we know which array each element comes from and can fetch the next element. The algorithm: 1) Initialize heap with first elements from all non-empty arrays. 2) While heap not empty: pop minimum, record it, if there's a next element in same array, push it. Time: Each of nk elements is pushed/popped once → O(nk log k). Space: O(k) for heap. Connection to L0: This is the engine that drives the full merge algorithm. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of comparing across arrays. Common mistakes: Not storing array indices, incorrect heap ordering, forgetting to check if arrays are empty. Real code pattern: heap = [], for i,arr in enumerate(arrays): if arr: heapq.heappush(heap, (arr[0], i, 0)).

examples.trace: Initial: Push (1,0,0), (2,1,0), (3,2,0). Heap: [(1,0,0),(2,1,0),(3,2,0)]. Step 1: Pop min (1,0,0). Next in array 0 is index 1 → push (4,0,1). Heap: [(2,1,0),(3,2,0),(4,0,1)]. Step 2: Pop min (2,1,0). Next in array 1 is index 1 → push (5,1,1). Heap: [(3,2,0),(4,0,1),(5,1,1)]. Step 3: Pop min (3,2,0). Next in array 2 is index 1 → push (6,2,1). Heap: [(4,0,1),(5,1,1),(6,2,1)]. Step 4: Pop min (4,0,1). Array 0 exhausted. Heap: [(5,1,1),(6,2,1)]. Step 5: Pop min (5,1,1). Array 1 exhausted. Heap: [(6,2,1)]. Step 6: Pop min (6,2,1). Array 2 exhausted. Heap empty.

Initial: Push (10,0,0), (5,1,0), (20,2,0). Heap: [(5,1,0),(10,0,0),(20,2,0)]. Step 1: Pop min (5,1,0). Next in array 1 is index 1 → push (15,1,1). Heap: [(10,0,0),(20,2,0),(15,1,1)]. Step 2: Pop min (10,0,0). Array 0 exhausted. Heap: [(15,1,1),(20,2,0)]. Step 3: Pop min (15,1,1). Array 1 exhausted. Heap: [(20,2,0)]. Step 4: Pop min (20,2,0). Array 2 exhausted. Heap empty.

testCases.expected: Heap pushes: (1,0,0),(3,1,0); pops: 1,3; pushes after pops: (2,0,1),(4,1,1); final pops: 2,4
Heap pushes: (5,0,0),(1,1,0),(2,2,0); pops: 1,2,5; pushes after pops: (9,1,1); final pops: 9
Heap pushes: (10,0,0),(15,1,0); pops: 10,15; pushes after pops: (20,0,1); final pop: 20
Heap pushes: (100,0,0),(50,1,0); pops: 50,100; pushes after pops: (150,1,1),(200,0,1); final pops: 150,200
Heap pushes: (1,0,0),(2,1,0); pops: 1,2,3,4,5,6 with appropriate pushes
Heap pushes: (7,0,0),(8,1,0),(9,2,0); pops: 7,8,9; no pushes after pops
Heap pushes: (1,1,0); pops: 1; pushes after pops: (2,1,1); final pop: 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Across Multiple Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Understand how to efficiently find the minimum element across k sorted arrays.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that when merging sorted arrays, we only need to compare the current front elements of each array since they're the smallest remaining in their respective arrays. The core technique is maintaining a collection of these front elements and repeatedly picking the smallest one. Without optimization, comparing all k elements each time would be O(k) per element → O(nk²). The min-heap reduces this to O(log k) by keeping elements in a heap structure. The algorithm: 1) Identify current elements from each array. 2) Find minimum among them. 3) Remove that minimum and advance in its array. 4) Repeat. Time: Finding minimum with heap is O(log k) vs O(k) with linear scan. Space: O(k) to store current elements. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented using heap operations. Connection to L3: This builds on understanding of sorted order. Common mistakes: Comparing entire arrays instead of just front elements, not updating pointers correctly. Real code pattern: Initialize pointers array [0]*k, while any array has elements: find min among arr[i][ptr[i]], increment ptr[min_index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Current elements: [1,2,3]. Minimum is 1 (from array 0). Advance pointer 0 to 1. Step 2: Current elements: [4,2,3]. Minimum is 2 (from array 1). Advance pointer 1 to 1. Step 3: Current elements: [4,5,3]. Minimum is 3 (from array 2). Advance pointer 2 to 1. Step 4: Current elements: [4,5,6]. Minimum is 4 (from array 0). Advance pointer 0 to 2 (exhausted). Step 5: Current elements: [None,5,6]. Minimum is 5 (from array 1). Advance pointer 1 to 2 (exhausted). Step 6: Current elements: [None,None,6]. Minimum is 6 (from array 2). Advance pointer 2 to 2 (exhausted). Done.

Step 1: Current elements: [10,5]. Minimum is 5 (from array 1). Advance pointer 1 to 1. Step 2: Current elements: [10,15]. Minimum is 10 (from array 0). Advance pointer 0 to 1. Step 3: Current elements: [20,15]. Minimum is 15 (from array 1). Advance pointer 1 to 2 (exhausted). Step 4: Current elements: [20,None]. Minimum is 20 (from array 0). Advance pointer 0 to 2 (exhausted). Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Sorted Order and Merging
ladders.desc: Learn how merging leverages the sorted property of individual arrays.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that if each array is sorted, then the smallest element overall must be the smallest among the first elements of all arrays. This is because in a sorted array, the first element is guaranteed to be ≤ all other elements in that array. Therefore, by repeatedly taking the smallest available first element, we build the merged result in sorted order. The key property: For sorted arrays A and B, if A[i] ≤ A[i+1] and B[j] ≤ B[j+1], then when merging, we only need to compare A[i] and B[j] to decide which comes next. Extending to k arrays: the global minimum is the minimum among all A[0] values. After removing it, the next global minimum is the minimum among the new set of front elements. Time: This insight reduces the problem from comparing all elements to comparing only k front elements. Space: Just need to track k positions. Connection to L2: This understanding enables the cross-array comparison logic. Connection to L4: This relies on basic comparison operations. Common mistakes: Not realizing sorted property eliminates need to compare non-front elements, trying to merge by concatenating and re-sorting. Real code pattern: result = [], while arrays have elements: find min first element, append to result, remove from that array.

examples.trace: Step 1: Front elements: 1 and 2. Minimum is 1. Result: [1]. Array1 becomes [4,7]. Step 2: Front elements: 4 and 2. Minimum is 2. Result: [1,2]. Array2 becomes [5,8]. Step 3: Front elements: 4 and 5. Minimum is 4. Result: [1,2,4]. Array1 becomes [7]. Step 4: Front elements: 7 and 5. Minimum is 5. Result: [1,2,4,5]. Array2 becomes [8]. Step 5: Front elements: 7 and 8. Minimum is 7. Result: [1,2,4,5,7]. Array1 becomes []. Step 6: Front elements: None and 8. Minimum is 8. Result: [1,2,4,5,7,8]. Array2 becomes []. Done.

Step 1: Front elements: 3,1,2. Minimum is 1. Result: [1]. Array2 becomes []. Step 2: Front elements: 3,None,2. Minimum is 2. Result: [1,2]. Array3 becomes []. Step 3: Front elements: 3,None,None. Minimum is 3. Result: [1,2,3]. Array1 becomes []. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Selection
ladders.desc: Practice comparing elements and selecting the minimum among multiple values.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that at the heart of merging is repeatedly finding the smallest value from a set of candidates. This is a fundamental operation that appears in many algorithms. For k elements, finding the minimum requires comparing elements pairwise. The algorithm: 1) Initialize min_value = first element, min_index = 0. 2) For each subsequent element, if it's smaller than min_value, update min_value and min_index. 3) Return both min_value and its source index. Time: O(k) for linear scan, which motivates using a heap for better efficiency in the full problem. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to find the minimum front element. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding of comparison operators. Common mistakes: Forgetting to track the index along with value, not handling empty sets, incorrect comparison logic for edge cases. Real code pattern: min_val = candidates[0], min_idx = 0; for i in range(1,len(candidates)): if candidates[i] < min_val: min_val = candidates[i]; min_idx = i.

examples.trace: Step 1: min_val=5, min_idx=0. Step 2: Compare 2 < 5 → update min_val=2, min_idx=1. Step 3: Compare 8 < 2? No. Step 4: Compare 1 < 2 → update min_val=1, min_idx=3. Final: min_value=1 at index 3.
Step 1: min_val=10, min_idx=0. Step 2: Compare 10 < 10? No. Step 3: Compare 5 < 10 → update min_val=5, min_idx=2. Step 4: Compare 5 < 5? No. Final: min_value=5 at index 2 (first occurrence).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn about arrays, accessing elements, and comparing numbers.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections of elements where each element has an index starting from 0. To work with multiple arrays, we need to understand how to access elements using indices and compare values using comparison operators (<, >, =). This is the absolute prerequisite for any merging algorithm. The algorithm: 1) Access array element: array[index]. 2) Compare two numbers: a < b returns True if a is smaller. 3) Find minimum of two numbers: min(a,b) or manual comparison. Time: O(1) for access and comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This provides the building blocks for comparing multiple elements. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, confusing array values with indices, incorrect comparison logic. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; first_element = arr[0]; second_element = arr[1]; if first_element < second_element: print('first is smaller').

examples.trace: Step 1: arr[0] = 10. Step 2: arr[1] = 20. Step 3: arr[2] = 30. Comparisons: 10 < 20 is True, 20 < 30 is True, 30 < 10 is False.
Step 1: 5 > 3 evaluates to True because 5 is greater than 3. Step 2: 5 < 3 evaluates to False. Step 3: 5 == 3 evaluates to False. The minimum is 3.

approaches.name: Divide and Conquer O(nk log k)
approaches.summary: Recursively merge pairs of arrays using a two-pointer merge, similar to the merge step in merge sort. This reduces the problem size logarithmically and avoids the overhead of a heap, making it efficient in practice.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Merge k Sorted Arrays Using Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Complete solution merging k sorted arrays using recursive pairwise merging
ladders.explanation: The divide and conquer approach to merging k sorted arrays works by recursively splitting the problem into smaller subproblems, then combining results. Intuitively, instead of trying to merge all arrays at once (which would be complex), we treat it like the merge step in merge sort: we recursively merge pairs of arrays until we have one final merged array. This works because merging two sorted arrays is straightforward using the two-pointer technique, and by applying this recursively, we reduce the k-way merge problem to multiple 2-way merges. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if there's only one array, return it. 2) Split the list of arrays into two halves. 3) Recursively merge each half. 4) Merge the two resulting sorted arrays using two-pointer merge. Time complexity is O(nk log k) where n is average array length and k is number of arrays - each element participates in log k merge operations. Space complexity is O(nk) for storing intermediate results. Connection to L1: This uses the two-pointer merge subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes: not handling empty arrays properly, incorrect recursion termination, inefficient array copying. Real pattern: def merge_k_arrays(arrays): if len(arrays) == 1: return arrays[0]; mid = len(arrays)//2; left = merge_k_arrays(arrays[:mid]); right = merge_k_arrays(arrays[mid:]); return merge_two_arrays(left, right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Split into [[1,4,7], [2,5,8]] and [[3,6,9]]. Step 2: Recursively merge left half: split [[1,4,7], [2,5,8]] into [[1,4,7]] and [[2,5,8]]; base cases return [1,4,7] and [2,5,8]; merge them: pointers at 1 and 2 → add 1; pointers at 4 and 2 → add 2; pointers at 4 and 5 → add 4; pointers at 7 and 5 → add 5; pointers at 7 and 8 → add 7; add remaining 8 → result [1,2,4,5,7,8]. Step 3: Recursively merge right half: [[3,6,9]] returns [3,6,9]. Step 4: Merge [1,2,4,5,7,8] and [3,6,9]: pointers at 1 and 3 → add 1; pointers at 2 and 3 → add 2; pointers at 4 and 3 → add 3; pointers at 4 and 6 → add 4; pointers at 5 and 6 → add 5; pointers at 7 and 6 → add 6; pointers at 7 and 9 → add 7; pointers at 8 and 9 → add 8; add remaining 9 → final [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9].

Step 1: Split into [[10,20], []] and [[5,15,25]]. Step 2: Recursively merge left half: split [[10,20], []] into [[10,20]] and [[]]; base cases return [10,20] and []; merge them: pointers at 10 and (none) → add all from first: [10,20]. Step 3: Recursively merge right half: [[5,15,25]] returns [5,15,25]. Step 4: Merge [10,20] and [5,15,25]: pointers at 10 and 5 → add 5; pointers at 10 and 15 → add 10; pointers at 20 and 15 → add 15; pointers at 20 and 25 → add 20; add remaining 25 → final [5,10,15,20,25].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Arrays Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Efficiently combine two sorted arrays into one sorted array
ladders.explanation: Merging two sorted arrays is fundamental to the divide and conquer approach. Intuition: since both arrays are sorted, we can compare the smallest remaining elements from each array and always pick the smaller one, guaranteeing the merged result stays sorted. This is like the merge step in merge sort. Algorithm: 1) Initialize two pointers i and j at the start of each array. 2) Create an empty result list. 3) While both pointers are within bounds: compare elements at i and j; append the smaller element to result and move that pointer forward. 4) After one array is exhausted, append all remaining elements from the other array. Time complexity: O(m+n) where m and n are array lengths, as each element is processed once. Space complexity: O(m+n) for the result array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the recursive merging process. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle remaining elements, off-by-one errors in pointer movement, not preserving stability if needed. Real pattern: def merge_two_arrays(arr1, arr2): result = []; i=j=0; while i<len(arr1) and j<len(arr2): if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i+=1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j+=1; result.extend(arr1[i:]); result.extend(arr2[j:]); return result.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Pairwise Reduction
ladders.desc: Divide k arrays into halves and recursively merge until one remains
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the divide and conquer approach is reducing a k-way problem to multiple 2-way problems through recursion. Intuition: merging k arrays directly is complex, but if we recursively merge pairs, we build up the solution from smaller, manageable pieces—similar to how merge sort divides sorting into smaller sorts. This logarithmic reduction (log k levels) is efficient. Algorithm: 1) Base case: if only one array, return it. 2) Find midpoint to split arrays into left and right halves. 3) Recursively call merge on left half and right half. 4) Merge the two results using the two-pointer merge. Time complexity arises from log k levels of recursion, each involving O(nk) work in total. Space complexity: O(nk) for recursion stack and intermediate arrays. Connection to L1: This logic uses the two-pointer merge as its combining step. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if base case incorrect, inefficient splitting causing imbalance, not handling empty input. Real pattern: def recursive_merge(arrays): if len(arrays) <= 1: return arrays[0] if arrays else []; mid = len(arrays)//2; left = recursive_merge(arrays[:mid]); right = recursive_merge(arrays[mid:]); return merge_two_arrays(left, right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Not base case (3 arrays). Split at mid=1: left=[[1,4,7]], right=[[2,5,8], [3,6,9]]. Step 2: Recursively process left: base case returns [1,4,7]. Step 3: Recursively process right: split [[2,5,8], [3,6,9]] into left2=[[2,5,8]] and right2=[[3,6,9]]; base cases return [2,5,8] and [3,6,9]; merge them: [2,3,5,6,8,9]. Step 4: Merge [1,4,7] and [2,3,5,6,8,9] → final [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9].

Step 1: Not base case (2 arrays). Split at mid=1: left=[[10,20]], right=[[5,15,25]]. Step 2: Recursively process left: base case returns [10,20]. Step 3: Recursively process right: base case returns [5,15,25]. Step 4: Merge [10,20] and [5,15,25] → final [5,10,15,20,25].

ladders.title: Building Block — Splitting and Combining in Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Understanding how to break a problem into subproblems and merge solutions
ladders.explanation: Divide and conquer is a paradigm where a problem is recursively broken down into smaller subproblems until they become simple enough to solve directly, then solutions are combined to solve the original problem. Intuition: complex problems can often be solved by dividing them into similar but smaller problems—like splitting a list in half repeatedly. For merging k arrays, we split the list of arrays into halves, solve each half (by further splitting or merging), then combine the sorted results. Key aspects: 1) Division: choosing how to split (e.g., midpoint for arrays). 2) Base case: when to stop recursion (e.g., single array). 3) Combination: how to merge sub-solutions (e.g., two-pointer merge). Time/space depends on division factor and combination cost. Connection to L2: This general concept is applied specifically to array merging. Common mistakes: splitting inefficiently (not halving), incorrect base case, not combining properly. Real pattern: def divide_conquer(problem): if base_case(problem): return solve_directly(problem); subproblems = split(problem); subsolutions = [divide_conquer(sub) for sub in subproblems]; return combine(subsolutions).

examples.input: Split list [A,B,C,D] into halves and combine
examples.trace: Step 1: Problem: process [A,B,C,D]. Step 2: Split into left=[A,B] and right=[C,D]. Step 3: Recursively process left: split [A,B] into [A] and [B]; base cases solve A and B; combine A and B → result1. Step 4: Recursively process right: split [C,D] into [C] and [D]; base cases solve C and D; combine C and D → result2. Step 5: Combine result1 and result2 → final result.

examples.input: Split list [X,Y] into halves and combine
examples.trace: Step 1: Problem: process [X,Y]. Step 2: Split into left=[X] and right=[Y]. Step 3: Recursively process left: base case returns X. Step 4: Recursively process right: base case returns Y. Step 5: Combine X and Y → final result.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Appending Elements in Order
ladders.desc: Fundamental step of selecting the smaller element between two sorted sequences
ladders.explanation: At the heart of merging sorted arrays is the operation of comparing two elements and appending the smaller one to a result list, preserving sorted order. Intuition: when you have two sorted lists, the smallest overall element must be the smaller of the two current front elements—by repeatedly picking this minimum, you build a sorted merged list. This is a greedy choice that guarantees correctness because the lists are sorted. Step-by-step: 1) Look at the current elements from each list (using pointers). 2) Compare them. 3) Append the smaller element to the result. 4) Move the pointer in the list from which you took the element. Repeat until one list is empty. Time per operation: O(1) for comparison and append. Space: O(1) per element added. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the combine step of divide and conquer. Common mistakes: comparing incorrectly (e.g., using > instead of <), not moving the correct pointer, handling ties improperly. Real pattern: if arr1[i] <= arr2[j]: result.append(arr1[i]); i+=1 else: result.append(arr2[j]); j+=1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Current elements: arr1[i]=1, arr2[j]=2. Step 2: Compare: 1 <= 2 is true. Step 3: Append 1 to result. Step 4: Move i forward (i=1). Result so far: [1].
Step 1: Current elements: arr1[i]=10, arr2[j]=5. Step 2: Compare: 10 <= 5 is false. Step 3: Append 5 to result. Step 4: Move j forward (j=1). Result so far: [5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays and Element Order
ladders.desc: Basic prerequisite: what it means for an array to be sorted and how to traverse it
ladders.explanation: A sorted array is a sequence where elements are arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is less than or equal to the next). Intuition: this property allows efficient operations like searching or merging because you know the relative positions of elements. For example, in [1,4,7], 1 is the smallest, then 4, then 7. Traversing means accessing elements one by one from start to end, typically using an index or pointer. Key concepts: 1) Order: elements increase or stay the same as you move forward. 2) Indexing: positions start at 0. 3) Length: number of elements. Time to access an element: O(1). Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: Merging relies on the fact that arrays are sorted to compare elements efficiently. Common mistakes: confusing sorted with other orders, off-by-one in indexing, not handling empty arrays. Real pattern: arr = [1,4,7]; first_element = arr[0]; second_element = arr[1]; etc.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check order: 2 <= 5 is true, 5 <= 8 is true, so sorted. Step 2: Traverse: index 0 has 2, index 1 has 5, index 2 has 8. Step 3: Length is 3.
Step 1: No elements to check order. Step 2: Length is 0. Step 3: It is trivially sorted.
